title: practical english usage - Part 174
content:
for example in if-clauses, after comparisons, and together with adverbs, verbs, sentences, but in other cases where we  are not making affirmative statements - Non-affirmative words are common not only in questions and negative Have you ever met the Prime Minister?

- I met the Prime Minister once.

Do you ever go to the theatre?

- I sometimes go to the theatre.

- She's already here.

I haven't bought you anything.

- I've bought you something.

Did anybody telephone?

- Somebody telephoned.

somebody, once, sometimes, already. Compare: that something is true) we  normally use other words - for example some, example any, anybody, ever, yet. When we  affirm or assert (that is, when we say There are some words that are not often used in affirmative sentences - for y et,   et c __ _____________            __   _ 222 non-affirmative          words:    anybody,         ever, Basic Clause Types    Section  19 grammar •  223   exclamations not He's such a nice boy! (     ... a such nice boy!) such a/an (+ adjective) + singular countable noun You're so kind!

3  exclamations          with    so  and   such not What a beautiful smile your sister has! (     ... has your sister!) what + object + subject + verb (note word order) What lovely flowers! What fools!

What beautijiil weather! (     What-a-beautiful weather!) what (+ adjective) + uncountable/plural noun What a surprise!

What a nice dress! (     What nice dress!) not What a rude man! (     What-rude-man!) what a/an (+ adjective) + singular countable noun 2 exclamations          with    what For the structure of expressions like How strange a remark, ►     187.

how  + subject + verb not How beautifully you sing! (     How-you-sing-beautijully!) not How cold it is! (     How it is cold!) how + adjective/adverb + subject + verb Strawberries! How nice!

These are often felt to be a little formal or old-fashioned.

1  exclamations          with    how negative question forms are also common.

Exclamations are often constructed with how and what or with so and such; 223 exclamatio       ns  _______________________ grammar •  224  imperatives Do be quiet!

Do be can begin emphatic imperatives.

negative imperatives.

Although do is not normally used as an auxiliary with be (►    19), this happens in 4 do(n't)    be For more about get as passive auxiliary, ►    60.

Get vaccinated as soon as you can.

get + past participle.

To  tell people to arrange for things to be done to them, we  often use 3  passive      imperative:       Get   vaccinated.

Do sit down. Do be more careful. Do forgive me.

We   can make an emphatic imperative with do (mainly BrE).

2 emphatic       imperative:       Do   sit   down.

Don't do that again or you'll be in trouble.

Shut up or I'll lose my temper. (= If you don’ t shut up ...) Walk down our street any day and you'll see kids playing. (= If you walk ...) An imperative followed by and or or can mean the same as an if-clause.

Try again - you nearly did it. Have some more tea. Enjoy your break.

Tell him  you're not free this evening.

Please do not lean out of the window.

Look in the mirror before you drive off.

express wishes for people’ s welfare.

suggestions, to give advice or instructions, to encourage and offer, and to Imperatives are used, for example, to tell or ask people to do things, to make are constructed with do not (don't).

imperatives have the same form as the infinitive without to; negative imperatives verb forms come, be, have and don't worry are called ‘ imperatives'. Affirmative In sentences like Come here, Be quiet, Have a drink or Don't worry about it, the 1  forms     and   use 224 imperatives For more information about negative questions, ►    218.

Boy, am I hungry! Wow, did she make a mistake! Was I furious!

forms in exclamations.

Americans and some British speakers may use ordinary (non-negative) question Isn't the weather nice! Hasn't she grown!

4 negative       question       forms For more information about such and so, ►    597.

They're such kind people! (     .. . so kind people!) not They talk such rubbish! (     ... such a rubbish!) such (+ adjective) + uncountable/plural noun Basic Clause Types    Section 19 grammar •  225  let introducing imperatives Don't let's stay up too late tonight. (informal) Let's not get angry. (informal) Do not let us forget those who came before us. (formal) Let us not despair. (formal) Negatives are let us not / do not let us (formal); let's not / don't let's (informal).
